q1.question: Do I need a business idea to start?
q1.answer: No — most people start with the assessment and let BusinessDNA suggest matches based on their profile.

q2.question: What languages are supported?
q2.answer: The app and every generated document are available in English and Romanian, with more languages planned.

q3.question: Can I cancel anytime?
q3.answer: Yes, subscriptions are month-to-month with no long-term commitment.

q4.question: Is my data kept private?

hero.summary: Based on your answers so far, you show strong alignment with {archetype} traits — comfort with ambiguity, a pull toward new ideas, and a bias for bold moves over safe ones.

systemsBuilder.title: Systems Builder
systemsBuilder.description: You turn ideas into processes that keep running without you in the room.
systemsBuilder.summary: You think in workflows and checklists more than grand gestures — your edge comes from building things that hold up under repetition, not just from a single good idea.

visionaryOperator.title: Visionary Operator
visionaryOperator.description: You pair a long view of where things are headed with the discipline to actually run the day-to-day.
visionaryOperator.summary: You're rare in combining big-picture thinking with operational follow-through — you can set the direction and still show up to execute it.

creativeStrategist.title: Creative Strategist
creativeStrategist.description: You lead with original ideas, then work backward into a plan that makes them real.
creativeStrategist.summary: Your instinct is to imagine the unconventional option first, then apply structure — that ordering is what tends to set your work apart.

growthArchitect.title: Growth Architect
growthArchitect.description: You're drawn to finding the mechanisms that make a business compound, not just start.
growthArchitect.summary: You naturally look past the launch toward what makes something scale — the channel, the loop, the second and third order effect.

executionSpecialist.title: Execution Specialist
executionSpecialist.description: You're most energized by shipping — turning a plan into something real, fast.
executionSpecialist.summary: Where others deliberate, you tend to move — your strength is closing the gap between a decision and a finished result.

revenueForecast.notes: Forecast assumes steady retainer-client growth, not a viral spike — typical of referral-driven accounting practices.

breakEven.notes: Structural estimate only — actual break-even depends on client-acquisition pace.

grossMargin.notes: Estimate once a part-time bookkeeper is on staff; solo-founder margins run higher.

netMargin.notes: After software, insurance, continuing education, and staff wages.

scenarios.conservative: 6 retainer clients at the base tier, slow referral flow.
scenarios.expected: 12 retainer clients across mixed tiers plus seasonal tax filing income.
scenarios.optimistic: 18+ retainer clients plus an active fractional-CFO engagement.

risks.risk1: A large client leaving mid-year creates an immediate revenue gap.
risks.risk2: Tax-season staffing costs spike faster than tax-season revenue.

item1.title: Selling a fractional-CFO tier to your best existing retainer clients is cheaper than acquiring new ones.

item2.title: E-commerce sellers are chronically underserved by generalist bookkeepers and pay a premium for inventory-aware accounting.

item1.title: A single missed quarterly estimated-tax deadline for a client can end the relationship instantly, regardless of your track record.
Pricing by the hour instead of by transaction volume, which punishes efficiency.

item2.title: Taking on clients in industries the firm doesn't understand (e.g., inventory-heavy retail) without adjusting the process.

item1.title: Offering a free books-health assessment converts warm referrals faster than any cold outreach.
Standardize the close checklist before hiring — otherwise every new hire reinvents the process.
Cap any single client at under 20% of monthly recurring revenue to avoid concentration risk.
Firms that publish their close-date guarantee publicly close more referral deals than those that don't.
Most small-business owners switch accountants not over price, but over slow response times.
Continuing-education credit renewal fees are easy to forget when budgeting the first year.
Demand for e-commerce and multi-currency bookkeeping is rising faster than demand for traditional single-location retail bookkeeping.
A one-person practice reached 25 retainer clients within 2 years by referral alone, with zero paid advertising.
A practice that took on too many low-margin, high-transaction-volume clients burned out its only bookkeeper within a year.

q1.question: Do I need a physical office to start?
q1.answer: No — most client work can be done remotely through a cloud practice-management portal.

myth1.myth: AI will make bookkeepers obsolete within a few years.
myth1.fact: AI automates the transactional categorization work, but advisory judgment and client trust remain human-led for the foreseeable future.

revenueForecast.notes: Forecast assumes slow early growth that compounds as AUM and referrals build over years, not a fast ramp.

breakEven.notes: Structural estimate only — actual break-even depends heavily on referral-network development speed.

grossMargin.notes: High margin once AUM is established, since incremental client servicing cost is low.

netMargin.notes: After compliance, insurance, and software costs.

scenarios.conservative: A handful of early clients, AUM still below the first meaningful threshold.
scenarios.expected: Steady referral flow, AUM past €5M with a mix of AUM and flat-fee clients.
scenarios.optimistic: Strong centers-of-influence network and a junior advisor generating additional fee-split revenue.

risks.risk1: A prolonged market downturn reduces AUM-based fee revenue across the whole client base at once.
risks.risk2: Regulatory or licensing changes can require costly compliance updates.

aiMetadata.generationHints: Model revenue as slow-building and AUM-dependent, sensitive to market cycles.
